, A GUIDE TO BOOKS ON IRELAND.
g

1 should like to call the attention of those interested in Irish books ik 1
to a project on which I have been engaged for some time past, viz., the g i
compilation of a Reader’s Guide to Books on Ireland. Before explain- 8
ing the nature of this undertaking, I should wish to forestall wrong gi
impressions by saying at once what it is not. In the first place it does o “J
not lay claim to be a bibliography. By this I do not mean that T am
content to be inaccurate or haphazard, but simply that my aim is not
exhaustive completeness. Secondly, I do not include merely books by
Irish writers, and thirdly, not merely books printed in Ireland. My
idea was this, to get together in a handy workable form a carefully
classified list of books dealing with Ireland and the Irish, each title

included to Dbe accompanied by a descriptive note, brief, but not too
scanty,

el

general reader, who wishes to study Ireland. It might also be of much
assistance to those who have to prepare lectures, or speeches, or arti-
cles, and in particular the members of various national propagandist
O | Organizations, political and literary. Again, it might help such as have
Jl | to select books whether for libraries or for prizes. I have been assured
?}; | that publishers and booksellers  might find it useful in éﬂpious

PRINTING IN THE TOWN OF CAVAN.

Having been recently asked a question as to printing in Cavan

by Mr. Charles Sayle, of University Library, Cambridge, who is at
present engaged cataloguing the very valuable collection of books re-
lating to Ireland gathered by the late Henry Bradshaw and by him
lJest-ow:ed on that Library, it occurs to me to put on record in this
magazine some particulars as to the printing in that town.

In the first place I might mention that the earliest item of print-

ing that I have seen at Cavan was a pamphlet entitled “ A List of the
Several Baronies and Parishes in the County of Cavan, together with
all the Denominations of Land in each Parish, al'phabe.t.icall*v arranged

work of George Russell. (A. E.) “ The Poet whose first volume came

ward : Songs by the way’’ was published by a gentleman who called
himself for that purpose ‘° Whalley,” and I do not know that
‘““ Whalley ’ ever published any other work. This book was not to he
had at the bookshops; it was necessary to seek out the publisher at an
out-of-the-way address in Dublin, and very difficult to find him at
home. Yet the book sold, kept on selling; amiable pirates came to its
aid in America, and ultimately it was re-issued in London by Mr. Lane,
but not until it had already become known to thousands. The un-
known young man who did not even care to set his name on the title-
page, had, in the by-times; of a working life, spent over ledgers in a
Dublin counting-house, produced harmonies which took possession of
chance hearers all over the English-speaking world.
